topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I reread the subject line three times, my eyes blurring over the familiar, unwelcome words. It was a notification about my piece, the one I’d poured months of late nights and sheer willpower into. The name attached to the publication was wrong. A different name. My hands trembled as I clicked into the article, scanning the introduction, the quoted material, the very sentences I had crafted. A slow, burning heat began to rise in my cheeks. I leaned back in my chair, the worn leather groaning in protest, and stared blankly at the wall, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

"No. This can't be happening." The words were a ragged whisper escaping my throat. I stood by the window, the city lights smeared across the glass, each one mocking the deception. My jaw was locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I walked over to my desk, the smooth wood feeling alien beneath my fingertips. I picked up the printed copy—a physical manifestation of the betrayal—and slowly, deliberately, I tore the page in half. The sound was sharp, violent, and I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to physically contain the sudden surge of corrosive disbelief churning in my gut. I needed air, needed distance, but every breath felt shallow, insufficient.

[story 3]

I sat at the kitchen table, the lukewarm coffee long gone cold in the mug. Every time I glanced at my phone, a wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim momentarily. It wasn't just the content; it was the theft of my voice, the unauthorized appropriation of my effort. My fingers kept tracing patterns on the condensation of the glass, seeking some anchor in the tangible world. A low, involuntary sound escaped me—a choked sigh that held all the frustration and hurt I couldn't articulate. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on tile sounding deafening in the quiet apartment. I needed to move, to shatter something, anything, to release the pressure building behind my sternum.
